source: Biyout, Elouadi & Mharzi (2018) – Drugs Circuit in the Hospital Pharmacy
title: Hospital Pharmacy BPM Process Model
description: Business Process Management model of the hospital pharmacy drug circuit, covering procurement, preparation, and availability. Derived from BPMN mapping of real hospital pharmacy operations.

procurement_decisional_chain.description: End-to-end decisional chain from suppliers to healthcare units

stages.name: Selection
stages.description: Pharmacist selects appropriate drugs based on therapeutic needs, formulary, cost-effectiveness, and availability.

stages.name: Acquisition
stages.description: Place orders with suppliers — planned (ordinary) and emergency (random) orders. Vendor selection, PO generation.

stages.name: Reception
stages.description: Receive shipments, verify against PO, check product integrity and documentation.

stages.name: Storage
stages.description: Drugs classified and stored by distribution automate. Classification by therapeutic class, controlled substance schedule, storage conditions.

stages.name: Storage Management
stages.description: Inventory monitoring, expiry tracking, cycle counts, reorder triggering. Paper highlights incompatibility between automate display data and actual component identification.
known_issues: Automate display data vs actual product mismatch
Difficult rapid identification of expired drugs
Resupply process stoppages from these dysfunctions

stages.name: Distribution
stages.description: Resupply care units on calendar schedule. Patient-specific dispensing for prescriptions sent directly to pharmacy.
stages.logistics: Each care unit has its own carrier for delivery

swot_analysis.description: SWOT analysis of the pharmaceutical supply chain procurement function
strengths: Pharmacist expertise in drug interactions and safety
weaknesses: System incompatibility between automate and inventory data
Complex multi-stakeholder environment (doctors, nurses, pharmacists, suppliers, admin)
Difficult identification of expired drugs in automated systems
opportunities: Automation and robotics for dispensing and inventory
AI/ML for demand forecasting and stock optimization
Barcoding and RFID for full traceability
Industrial engineering approaches adapted to healthcare
threats: Patient safety incidents from process failures
Budget cuts reducing pharmacy staff and technology investment

value_chain.description: Lean value chain of the procurement function, identifying transition points as highest-risk areas
primary_activities: Need identification → Selection → Order placement → Reception → Verification → Storage → Picking → Distribution → Dispensing
Each transition point between activities is a risk point that benefits from automation and standardization
support_activities: Facility management (clean rooms, cold chain, security)

key_actors.decision_authority: High — can approve or reject prescriptions, select suppliers, manage formulary
Medium — best operational knowledge of patient care, limited formal management power
High — controls funding but limited clinical understanding
External — influences via pricing, availability, lead times
